section_title: INTRODUCTION TO OTE PATTERN RECOGNITION VOL. 15
content:
Welcome to Volume 15 in a continuing series of 20 videos for the Optimal Trade Entry (OTE) Pattern
Recognition Series on the Inner Circle Trader YouTube channel.
- Our example today is going to be in the Australian Dollar versus the US Dollar (AUD/USD).
- The charts are already displayed on the 5-minute timeframe.

section_title: IDENTIFYING THE PRIMARY OTE ENTRY (8:30 AM NYC)
content:
Take a moment to study the chart before annotations are added. (Pause video now).
- Annotations have been added.
- We can observe our standard 8:30 AM to 11:00 AM New York Standard Time optimal trade entry.
- This price leg initiates precisely at 8:30 AM, moving from here to here and then down. A smaller
retracement also occurred, which was an optimal trade entry, but we must incorporate the specified time
window.

section_title: PRIMARY OTE ENTRY DETAILS AND INITIAL PERFORMANCE
content:
On this particular candle, at 8:30 AM dead on, price trades down.
- Your hypothetical fill on that candle would be at the 62% retracement level, consistent with the model's
minimum rules.
- Entry: 0.6619.
- Drawdown: 4 pips from your entry.
- Stop-loss: 10 pips below this low.

section_title: TARGET POTENTIAL AND THE RISK OF NOT TAKING PARTIALS (PRIMARY OTE)
content:
Observing an old high, the market returns almost 10 pips, then almost 20 pips.
- One standard deviation offers a handsome potential reward.
- One and a half standard deviations.
- Finally, price takes off at two standard deviations of the optimal trade entry range, offering 39 pips in this
short run.
- You have to weather this retracement, and it ultimately reaches the target. However, if you rigidly follow
the rules without taking parcels, you might bail out of the trade as it hits here, despite opportunities to take
parcels at any of these levels.

Here is a secondary entry opportunity.
- Using this price leg's low to the price leg's high, with the retracement down, your fill would be on this
candle, accounting for the spread.
- Hypothetical entry: 0.6639.
- Drawdown: only 3 pips.
- The subsequent run above the relative equal highs shows expansion.
- Ultimately, price trades to one-half of a standard deviation, offering more than 25 pips.

This candle trades exactly to the high of 0.66675 pipettes, and doesn't go any higher than that.
- It consolidates and breaks down. After trading down to here, it would have eventually stopped you out.
- Even on the initial entry, you would have trailed your stop-loss below these lows and gotten stopped out at
that point.
- Any partials left in the marketplace attempting to reach the previous day's high (0.66802 pipettes) would
have been for an undelivered target.

section_title: PROFIT VS. TARGET: THE IMPORTANCE OF PAYING YOURSELF
content:
As mentioned in today's Twitter dialogue, what's more important: your target being hit or profiting?
- Many traders lose money despite having open paper profit (not demo trading).
- How many times have you traded with live funds and watched a potential profit completely turn around
against you and go into a loss?
- Hypothetically, how many times could you go back in time and say, 'If I just would have took something off
when it was giving me X amount of pips or dollars of return?'
- As a general rule of thumb, you always pay yourself.

section_title: THE WORKWEEK ANALOGY AND CONSEQUENCES OF NEGLECTING PARTIALS
content:
Consider this workweek analogy: if you worked Monday and Tuesday of a normal workweek but became ill
and couldn't finish, does your employer get to keep all of those earned-income hours?
- Of course not. Similarly, when you put on a trade and the market offers an opportunity to take something
off (scaling), you have to do that.
- Otherwise, you fall victim to scenarios like this, where both patterns offered hypothetical opportunities to
book a profit.
- If you don't execute those partials at logical levels (as discussed with Fibonacci, which provides logical
levels, not magic), this is what will invariably happen if you keep doing this long enough.

section_title: OVERCOMING EMOTIONAL TRADING AND EMBRACING PARTIALS
content:
You will have a trade that looks good, strong, and performing, and it may even look like it's going to run up
above that high. But it peters out, loses momentum, and goes in the other direction.
- You're left with either being upset and emotional about not taking partials and getting stopped out, or
learning from that.
- Learn to say, 'Okay, I can take something off, and I'm not going to demand my best-case-scenario exit or
target. I'm going to take something off.'

There are many so-called educators in this industry who will say partials are stupid, dumb, or a rookie
attempt to satisfy the fear and greed tug-of-war.
- I agree with the last point: it rewards your time and risk.
- It doesn't matter that you're reducing the amount of potential profit; you're reducing, on a relative basis,
the same thing you had in terms of risk as you take something off.
- You're paying the trader; you're rewarding your initial risk and taking something out.
- Remember that workweek analogy: you have to find a way to grow comfortable taking something out of
the marketplace when it offers it to you.

section_title: ACHIEVING PROFITABILITY THROUGH STRATEGIC PARTIAL EXITS
content:
If you're unable to find profitability, and you can't warm up to this idea, I guarantee you if you try it, you'll
love it.
- It manages all of that fear and greed, that tug of war that takes place once you put a trade on in a live
setting.
- You want to get out of the trade, you want it to get to your target right away, but you can't force that.
- As it logically moves to levels where you could take partial profits and exit some of the position, you
reduce exposure or risk while rewarding and adding to your bottom line.
- These targets are not guaranteed, but if you're in a market that's trading here and you put your market
order to get out, that's more likely to fill you in a profit than waiting for that undelivered target.
- Both opportunities yielded the potential to pay out, but neither hit the logical level of the previous day's
high.
- It's not necessary to find profitability by getting your best-case scenario targets.
